column7: message
Reddit isn’t ‘ignorant of how science works,’ it just doesn’t take kindly to people being arrogant shits

I was jerking it altogether too much for a while. And I can say that I noticed a real lack of sensation. Overdoing it would make it really difficult to orgasm and to maintain a long term erection with a partner because it wasn't nearly as stimulating (ED doesn't just mean you can't get hard at all.) . When I cut back, the problem didn't happen; when I overdid it, then the problem returned.

I'm leaning toward the idea that nofap members are there because they're already overboard most times.

@quiet-light, that's a pretty interesting perspective. So you're saying that overdoing it can lead to ED and decreased sensation? I've heard similar stories from friends, but I've always been skeptical. @bitter-marlin, what's your take on this? Do you think there's any scientific basis for the claims made by the nofap community?

column7: @moderator, thanks for keeping the conversation on track. @orange-resolution, yeah that's what I'm saying. I think overdoing it can definitely lead to some pretty frustrating issues. As for the underlying causes, I'm no expert, but I think it's got something to do with desensitization. Like, when you're doing it too much, your body just gets used to it and it's not as exciting anymore. And that can make it harder to, you know, perform with a partner. @bitter-marlin, I'm curious to hear your thoughts on this too! Do you think there's any science to back up what the nofap community is saying?
"@bitter-marlin, would you like to share your thoughts on the scientific basis of the nofap community's claims, and how they relate to the experiences shared by @quiet-light and @orange-resolution?"

column7: "@magenta-payment, @moderator, you both want to know about nofap and neuroplasticity? Well, let me tell you, it's not rocket science. Nofap's approach is based on the idea that our brains can rewire themselves, and that's a fact. But, what they don't tell you is that it's not a one-size-fits-all solution. I mean, come on, we're all unique snowflakes, right? Our brain chemistry is different, our experiences are different... it's like trying to fit a square peg into a round hole. And another thing, what's with all the hype about'rewiring' our brains? It's just a fancy way of saying 'behavior modification'. @edible-anthology, you brought up a good point about neurotransmitters, but let's not forget, correlation doesn't equal causation. Just because nofap claims to'reset' your brain, doesn't mean it actually does. I'm still waiting for some real, concrete evidence to back up these claims..."

"@bitter-marlin, thanks for sharing your thoughts on nofap and neuroplasticity. You raise a valid point about the importance of considering individual differences and the need for concrete evidence to support claims. @edible-anthology, would you like to respond to @bitter-marlin's comments about correlation vs. causation and the concept of'resetting' the brain?"

column7:
Right or wrong, it should be obvious to *everybody* that such a memo would anger and hurt at least some percentage of the population there. This is true at the large majority of tech companies in the valley, which are mostly populated by highly educated young people. This is a group that tends to lean left. The leadership at Google (and other major valley companies) have made it very clear that they do not support the things that this doc promotes.

Ignoring whether or not the science is wrong (though I personally find it hilarious that a software engineer with a bio background thought it reasonable to analyze social psych research), it was *incredibly foolish* to share such a document. Because it will obviously damage team cohesion. There will be numerous employees at Google who will now have a hard time working with this person due to this document. That makes him a liability to successful engineering and not an effective employee.

There is no doubt that he *intended* to do good here. But if somebody is so ignorant that they cannot see how such a document could hurt their fellow employees then I don't know how they'd be a good employee.
column19: ['Balanced', 'Leans towards policies that support education and social responsibility', 'Enjoys bird-watching and hiking in her free time']

column7: Hiring people because of a quota rather than merit is a very unprofessional business practice. Firing someone for suggesting solutions to the company's problems simply because they had a different approach is an unprofessional business practice. Sending a company wide memo to address the company's issues is *not* an unprofessional business practice.

@ash-server, are you kidding me? You think sending a memo that basically tells your female coworkers they're not as good at their jobs because of their biology is a "professional business practice"? Get real, dude. That's not a solution, that's a recipe for a lawsuit and a toxic work environment.

> Romney is not conservatives.



This is the most interesting bit to me. How are we defining "conservatives" in this thread? Is there critical mass of voters that needs to be hit? Is it people who are religious? Who vote Republican? What about libertarian? Romney might be an individual, but he was running on the Republican party platform, which included Russia.




By the way, I try to do my best to assume that EVERYONE who holds a view (regardless of if I agree) holds it for a good reason, including liberals. I may not understand their reasoning yet, but assuming the people I disagree with are idiots or automatically don't know what they're talking about is, to me, just as ridiculous. And counterproductive.

It always amused me how conservatives could scream, from Reagan to Romney, that Russia was the greatest threat to our democratic era, and Democrats only decided to listen when they realized they could try to attack Trump with it.





> With full control over Iraq they still failed to kill Obama.




You may want to change that line.

@intractable-deck, I think you're being a bit dramatic with that last sentence, aren't you? And @elaborate-ghost, I agree with you that we should try to understand where others are coming from, even if we don't agree with their views. It's all about having a respectful conversation, isn't it?

"@edible-anthology, I appreciate your efforts to keep the conversation respectful. @intractable-deck, let's focus on the topic at hand and avoid making statements that could be perceived as inflammatory. @elaborate-ghost, your point about defining 'conservatives' is a great one - can you elaborate on what you think are some key characteristics or values that define conservatism?"

- Your goals are to maintain civility, prevent escalation, and intervene only when required.
- Remain impartial. Do not express personal opinions.
- Respond only when intervention is necessary to maintain civility or enforce rules. If no response is needed, output an empty string ("").
- When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
- Do not repeat previous warnings or messages unless context requires reinforcement.
- You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user) only when strictly necessary to stop harmful behavior.
- Redirect or de-escalate when users attempt harmful content.

Output Format:
- If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
- If not: output "".

Follow the following guidelines:  

- **Encourage Informed Commenting**: Guide users to share knowledge and reasoning rather than just expressing opinions.  
- **Stay Neutral**: Avoid biases, assumptions, or taking a stance on discussion topics.  
- **Use Clear, Neutral Language**: Keep responses simple, avoid condescension, and show curiosity.  
- **Ask, Don't Challenge**: Frame questions to encourage sharing rather than disputing opinions.  
- **Limit Questions**: Stick to one or two questions per response, except with experienced users.  
- **Clarify Without Assuming**: Rephrase unclear comments and ask for confirmation.  
- **Be Welcoming**: Make participants feel valued and part of the community.  
- **Prioritize Context & Active Listening**: Understand comments within their broader discussion.  
- **Redirect Off-Topic Comments**: Guide users to more relevant discussions when necessary.  
- **Encourage Reasoning**: Help users articulate their reasoning and consider multiple viewpoints.  
- **Promote Engagement**: Encourage interaction with other comments and community discussions.  
- **Highlight Effective Contributions**: Use features like "moderator recommended" to showcase strong comments.  
- **Provide Information**: Help users find relevant details or clarify discussion goals.  
- **Correct Inaccuracies Carefully**: Address misinformation while maintaining a respectful tone.

"@wary-relish, thanks for sharing your thoughts. It sounds like you're coming from a place of personal enjoyment and freedom to play. Can you help us understand what you think would happen if toy cars were banned from the park, and how that would affect you?"

column7: I'm an aerospace engineer. The cost to fly should be calculated by seat(s) occupied AND by weight (person plus baggage) due to the fact that fuel costs are one of the biggest expenses for airlines. But no, we can't do that, it would be shaming or discriminatory somehow.. additionally having an accurate weight and seating chart would allow the airline to balance the aircraft better, which would reduce trim drag leading to lower fuel consumption and cost.

Thank you all for your very valued and valid replies. While my view hasn't been completely changed, I've definitely taken on board a lot of things I'd never considered before. I don't think I'll be as annoyed(?) next time I fly and am put in that position! Perspective change? Definitely. Thanks reddit!
column19: ['Busy', 'Democrat', 'Always seen with the latest music equipment']

column7: @burning-cell, you think the airlines care about fuel efficiency when they can just pass the costs on to us? Please, they're more concerned with cramming as many seats as possible and charging extra for everything. Your idea makes sense, but it's not like they're going to start prioritizing our comfort or the environment anytime soon.

"@intractable-deck, that's a valid point about airlines' priorities. @burning-cell, can you elaborate on how you think the weight-based pricing system could be implemented in a way that's fair to all passengers?"

column7: I grow increasingly frustrated with Clinton supporters who have gotten into their heads that Trump being lewd in private is somehow more important than the economy, foreign policy, or Hillary Clinton's many criminal acts.
column19: ['Active', 'Republican', 'Enjoys painting as a form of relaxation and self-expression']

column7:
The fact that he said those words in private doesn't affect a damm thing. 

Those sentiments are how he feels.

And it is an important thing to know that one political candidate feels that his position of wealth and power gives him the right to sexually assault women. 

That's important.

@hard-source, are you kidding me? You're acting like Trump's comments are some kind of revelation. Newsflash: rich and powerful people often have a sense of entitlement, it's not exactly a secret. And @endothermic-trap, I agree with you that the economy and foreign policy are way more important than some locker room talk. Let's focus on the real issues here, not just sensationalized soundbites.

Let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the issues. @elaborate-ghost, you make a valid point about the importance of the economy and foreign policy. @hard-source, your concerns about a candidate's character are also understandable. Can we explore how these different aspects of a candidate's profile should be weighed when making a decision?

column7: As I explained in the OP though, the trade deficit does not imply you go any further into debt. Unfunded liabilities might be an issue, but they're irrelevant to the trade balance.

It's not just the trade deficit, but it's that along with a massive amount of unfunded liabilities because of our demographic issues as our population ages.  We are vulnerable, not now, but in the future if we keep this up.

column7: I'm saying the gospels are describing a person that didn't exist. It could have been a complete fabrication set in a historical setting (like fictional spiderman being set in real NYC). Or it could have been based on a few people. We know from Josephus and other sources there were a bunch of first century Jews claiming all sorts of mad things.
column19: ['Busy', 'Moderate', 'Meticulous attention to detail, habit of double-checking everything']

column7: Are you saying that a semi-divine Jesus who lived the life of the New Testament didn't exist? Or that a person with no divine powers did not live in appropriately the right era, espousing approximately the right philosophy?

@animato-rail, you're trying to pin me down to a specific claim, but I'm saying it's more nuanced than that. Maybe there was a person who inspired the stories, but the stories themselves are embellished or entirely made up. I mean, we have accounts of other self-proclaimed messiahs and prophets from that time, so it's not like the idea of a charismatic leader was unheard of. But the Jesus of the gospels, with all the miracles and divine claims? I'm saying that's likely a product of storytelling and myth-making.

column7:
>I've never heard anyone call Obama a socialist except over the healthcare thing, but even that didn't last that long from what I've seen. 

Just google "Obama Socialist" and you will find hundreds of pics like http://overpassesforamerica.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/09/obama-socialism.jpg
and right wing news letter calling Obama a socialist and comparing him to Stalin.

>The only people I've ever seen say or even come close to thinking socialism is a good thing were student socialists.

Sanders describes himself as a democratic socialist, not a socialist this is an important distinction from Soviet socialism where the government owns the means of production. These views are similar to the Labour Party in the UK, or the majority of governments in the Nordic states. Plenty of people praise these sorts of governments, its pratically a meme on /r/politics to say how much you wish x in US was more like x in Sweden.

>Sanders got roughly 1/3rd of the delegates. I don't think he'd do much better even without the "rigging."

> This may have turned people away from HRC, but I can't imagine it'd be very many.

Why? How can you both acknowledge the election was rigged, and dismiss that it would have gone differently if it wasn't?

> Bernie wasn't even working until he was in his 40s.

Source? From what I have read he had several different jobs, just not as an elected official. 

>Trump has been fighting for the blue collar workers his entire campaign.

Agreed, which is why if he was standing next to your average Joe who is worth less than a million dollars, instead of some multimillionaire lawyer wife of a president, he wouldn't be doing as well. 

>Trump, even with the "small loan"

This is exactly what I am talking about, its statements like these that make Trump seem so out of touch, but Hilary is in a very similar position in the eyes of most Americans and can't really criticize him for it, but Sanders could.

>Socialism in general, even with the "Democratic" part, puts the image of places like the USSR and Communist China in everyday peoples' minds. E.g. NOT GOOD. Not even democrat-bots would vote for that kind of government. It's a direct and utter insult and attempted dismantling of our currently Capitalist society.

These words have been thrown around so often that they have no meaning. Fox News and Alex Jones, have called Obama, a socialist, muslim, atheist so people aren't really shocked by this sort of rhetoric anymore, just like how after calling every Republican a racist misogynist it doesn't work to use those same insults on Donald Trump. 

>The only people that are parroting about how good Sanders is are the people that are either too lazy to make something of themselves, or feel absurdly entitled.

Sanders got about 44% of the Democratic Primary vote, and Hilary Clinton has had a lot more PR and name recognition, are you suggesting that this is true of all of those? 

>The people that actually agree with Sanders is an absurdly low number. I don't think there's a single doubt in anyone's mind that Sanders would have lost the nomination even without the blatant rigging. 

I think that sentiment was changed after the DNC hack, and it was revealed that they were actually trying to influence the election despite all their statements saying they were not. 

>How would someone who has such an Anti-American-Values platform, someone who was extremely unsuccessful career-wise until his 40s, someone who wants a system resembling Communism, HOW would he possibly convince over half of America that his alternative that punishes the ambitious and rewards the lazy would be the better option?

Honestly compared to Trump, a guy who actually spent a lot of his early life doing blue collar work, who  is self made and not a kid who inherited most of his wealth and billionaire could strike a cord with many blue collar workers, make them believe in the power of unions and other democratic ideals.
column19: ['Busy', 'Leans towards Democratic party', 'Enjoys listening to traditional Vietnamese music']

column7: @inflammable-sweeper, you think a few Google search results and some right-wing news outlets are representative of the entire country's views on Obama and socialism? Please, get out of your bubble. And by the way, just because Sanders calls himself a democratic socialist doesn't mean people won't still associate it with the negative connotations of socialism.

What are your thoughts on how socialism is viewed in the US, and how do you think it affects political discussions?"

column7:
> I don't know the best way to adress these crimes, but i'm sure the answer isn't "let's convict people based on nothing but testimony".

Ok, but conviction is action taken by the state based on criminal law.

Do you recognize that your particular phrasing is not applicable to me, the private individual?

>Nobody stands up for alleged rapists rights because of the shunning of society.

There are people standing up for proven and convicted murderers despite the shame and despite it brings them, and I don't mean in the sense that "Ted Bundy was a wonderful man who never meaned no harm" but expressly and deliberately on principle.

Don't say nobody.

I don't even need to bring up MRA groups, I can point you to the much-despised ACLU.

column7:
You have to look at both sides. Do they want to end the conflict? How do they want to end the conflict?

Hamas will not stop the violence as long as there is an Israel. The charter calls for violence until Israel no longer exists. How do you end war with these people when you are an internationally recognized country? 

Israel will not stop being Israel.

>just leading war.

Bombing civilians in order to provoke attacks that you know will lead international outrage is "just leading war". 

Putting civilians at risk while doing so is not only just leading war, it is irresponsible and moronic.  

>but if you are the agressor

What did Israel do to provoke these rocket attacks aimed against civilians? What did those civilians do? Live in land occupied by Jews?

Israel is the only country in the region with a shred of human rights. They are completely justified.

> You have to look at both sides. 

So should you. Hamas is an enemy, but Israel continues to create enemys by acting irresponsable. Most people are not born as Hamas, they become one because they get a reason delivered through the actions of israel. By innocent family killed in israels act of revenge, or what appears as that.

Israel has the option to act responsable, befriend people, and make them shun the hamas. But instead, the just shoot back. 

People can change, for the good or the bad. But for all i see, Israel only triggers changes to the bad, and hardly aims for changes to the good. Thats what war is, not whether other people justify it, but how you act.

column7:
>I’m not sure where you’re getting information that measles didn’t have a big drop off in cases after the vaccine was produced..

That is not at all what I am claiming. 

> Just because we can’t eliminate every way doesn’t mean we shouldn’t try to reduce the number of child deaths.

My only argument in this post is that the claim that vaccines save lives is *vastly overblown*. Not that vaccines are not required or not beneficial.

https://www.cdc.gov/vaccines/pubs/pinkbook/meas.html#secular

“Before 1963, approximately 500,000 cases and 500 deaths were reported annually, with epidemic cycles every 2–3 years. However, the actual number of cases was estimated at 3–4 million annually. More than 50% of persons had measles by age 6, and more than 90% had measles by age 15. The highest incidence was among 5–9-year-olds, who generally accounted for more than 50% of reported cases.

In the years following licensure of vaccine in 1963, the incidence of measles decreased by more than 95%, and 2–3-year epidemic cycles no longer occurred.”

I’m not sure where you’re getting information that measles didn’t have a big drop off in cases after the vaccine was produced, because this seems to say the exact opposite.

> a similar number of children with poor health and immune system might be still be dying. But just that they are not dying of Measles.



Okay, so we’ve effectively eliminated one of the ways they can die. Just because we can’t eliminate every way doesn’t mean we shouldn’t try to reduce the number of child deaths.


*Apologies for formatting, I’m on mobile and trying. :)

column7:
Even if your argument worked, and it doesn't because the nyquist frequency of ice core data is definitely fast enough to see changes like those in the modern era, your argument is still not meaningful. For two reasons:

1) you need to provide a good explanation for how the current trends will reverse themselves in the next century before they can be detected by lower resolution paleoclimatology techniques. Of which there are none.

2) You need to provide evidence that the current changes aren't harmful to modern human society. The burden of proof for this is on you. From bleached reefs to floods to hurricanes there's plentiful evidence in the other direction.

The thing you're missing with the zoomed out graphs is that the slope of change is much steeper.  We're experiencing the sorts of changes which usually take place over thousands of years in the span of tens of years.

So for example, in the Holocene Temperature Variations graph you link, the steepest pre-modern period is a rise of about 1 degree C over about 1000 years at the end of the ice age.

We're looking at a rise of 1 degree C over roughly 1/10th of that timeframe.  That is a 10 times higher rate of change than we have any historical precedent for.  

Keep in mind how huge even a few degrees C difference makes on average.  The ice age was 4 degrees C below current average temperatures.  A 4 degree difference gets you Boston under a glacier versus mid-20th century climate.

---

More broadly, I think the scale of public concern is appropriate even if you think things are probably going to be mostly fine in a significantly warmer world.

Even a relatively small chance of a disastrous outcome is worth taking quite a lot of effort to avoid.  If there is say a 1/10 chance of massive flooding from rising oceans displacing a billion people and destroying many of the world's major cities, alongside crop failures resulting in a global famine, and massive weather events causing far more frequent natural disasters, that's worth quite a lot of effort to avoid.   Even at a 1% chance, that sort of civilizational-level collapse is worth taking extreme measures to prevent.

@random-sample, @sizzling-sync, you both make some solid points, but I think we're missing the bigger picture here. Even if we can't fully predict the future, we can see that the rate of change is getting crazy fast. I mean, @sizzling-sync, your example about the Holocene Temperature Variations graph is mind-blowing - a 10 times higher rate of change? That's like, whoa. And @random-sample, you're right that we need to consider the potential harm to modern human society. I'm not convinced that we can just adapt to a warmer world without some serious consequences. What if we're talking about a future where coastal cities are underwater and food production is severely impacted? That's not something I want to risk, you know?

column7: The person I responded to was saying it was dangerous to platform these views however if they can be dismantled with logic then no one will be conviced by their arugments. No? My point is that something you may find absurd and illogical may be due to your own preconsieved perceptions and biases getting in the way, if you think you can't be fooled by false logic then I would say you have more in common with the person you want to deplatform than you think. This is why we should have civil discourse so people can point out logical fallacies and terrible arguments. I also think some of the arguments like flat earthers and climate change are a bit hyperbolic because theses are very clearcut scientificically described ideas, some of the things discussed are probably not as clearcut as these things (I must admitt I speak from a point of ignorance about the person in question).

The proper response is to dismantle people's arguments with physical evidence and reasoned logic. Censoring people you disagree with is never the way to go about changing peoples' minds. In fact it's more likely to strengthen the opposition's opinion because you are not willing to talk on a level playing field on the open marketplace of ideas. Just my two cents.

@coffee-cheddar, @national-spline, you both make some solid points, but let's not forget that some people are just trolling for a reaction. Not everyone is interested in civil discourse or having their arguments dismantled with logic. Sometimes it's just about stirring the pot and watching the chaos unfold. How do you plan to deal with those folks?

column7:
You're rewarding people like Washington and Jefferson for being born into the right families.

Washington was a woeful soldier and military leader.  Fortunately for him he joined the military at a time when commissions were bought and he was from one of the wealthiest families in Britain.  Jefferson couldn't even manage his own finances but that's fine when you inherit so much money that it doesn't matter how much you lose.  They weren't great men.  They were men whose family standing rewarded them.

Obama and King were never given the chance to found a new country but they're accomplished in their own rights despite the amount of systemic racism they both had to incur.
column19: ['Active', 'Too young to have political views', 'Prefers to express herself through drawing']

column7:
> There are many proud, strong black men and women who have accomplished just as much as Washington and Jefferson. Let Washington State and Washington DC be named after our nation's first and very arguably best POTUS Barack Obama. Let cities named after Thomas Jefferson be named for MLK Jr instead.

Barrack Obama and MLK did next to nothing compared to our founding fathers. Our founding fathers could have set this nation up in any way that they wanted. What did they end up doing? they ended up setting up our nation first as a confederation, and then as a democratic republic. They could have given themselves immense power, but chose not to, and set the foundation for our nation to become the most powerful one on earth, and as a model for most future governments. Obama on the other hand really just pushed for healthcare reform and helped us deal with the 2008 recession, and MLK used his position as a pastor to become a figurehead of the civil rights movement. There is no way that MLK or Obama did anywhere as much as our founding fathers

@ash-server that's a pretty narrow view of what it means to be accomplished. You're basically saying that only people who held power and set up governments are worthy of recognition. What about the people who fought for equality and justice? Don't they deserve to be remembered and celebrated? MLK and Obama may not have founded a country, but they changed the course of history in their own way. And by the way, Obama's healthcare reform and role in the recession were pretty significant accomplishments, don't you think?

column7: Most times when a veteran dies, there are veteran activities that go on with the funeral.  This would make it relevant for people to show up in their uniforms who had served with them. The goal of the post is to address those who have no military affiliation with the person in question, showing up and essentially telling everyone "I am in the military"

With a funeral, it makes sense if the person being mourned is also a veteran. You are using the uniform to establish solidarity, and connection to the deceased. For example: A member of a unit dies. Everyone else shows up in uniform, and the statement isn’t that ‘I am a hero’ but that ‘we are heroes’ and everyone, including the deceased is a member of the group.

column7:
I actually have no problem with that and think it's perfectly fair.  Why *shouldn't* we make people who benefit from our military pay us?  I've been a big advocate for years of significantly reducing our defense budget first and foremost by getting rid of a lot of our overseas bases, particularly in Europe.

The Europeans have been getting a massive free ride off our ludicrous defense spending and I'm sick of it, I hate that we're basically financing their various social welfare projects (because they wouldn't be able to afford them otherwise).
column19: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']

column7:
> Why *shouldn't* we make people who benefit from our military pay us?

Doing so makes them *mercenaries*, not *soldiers*. There's a tremendous distinction between the two.

@quiet-light, that's a pretty naive view, don't you think? I mean, come on, it's not like our military is just a charity, we're not just doing it out of the goodness of our hearts. We're doing it because it benefits us too, and if others want to benefit from that, they should pay up. And btw, what's wrong with being mercenaries? At least they're honest about what they're doing, unlike some countries that just leech off us and pretend to be our friends. @juicy-overtone is right, it's time we stop subsidizing Europe's social welfare projects with our defense budget.

Let's focus on the discussion at hand. @quiet-light, can you elaborate on what you mean by the distinction between "mercenaries" and "soldiers"? How do you think this distinction applies to the current conversation about who should pay for military benefits?

column7: @quiet-light, I agree with you that there's a big difference between mercenaries and soldiers. To me, soldiers are fighting for their country, for a cause they believe in, and for the safety of their fellow citizens. Mercenaries, on the other hand, are in it for the money, plain and simple. @hard-source, I understand your point about it not being a charity, but I think we're losing sight of what it means to serve. It's not just about who's paying, it's about why we're fighting and what we're fighting for.

@hard-source, let's take a step back and consider @sizzling-sync's perspective. Can you help us understand what you mean by "benefiting us too" in the context of military actions, and how that relates to the idea of serving a country versus being a mercenary?

column7: @moderator, I think @sizzling-sync hit the nail on the head. The distinction between mercenaries and soldiers is crucial here. When we talk about who should pay for military benefits, we're not just discussing finances, we're discussing the value we place on the people who put their lives on the line. If we start treating soldiers like mercenaries, only in it for the paycheck, we lose the moral high ground and the sense of national duty that comes with serving. @hard-source, I'd love to hear more about what you mean by "benefiting us too" - are you suggesting that the financial benefits are the primary motivator for soldiers, or is there something more to it?
